analogy: Think of "Life Processes" through the chapter's own opening images: Science 100 Control and Coordination 6 CHAPTER In the previous chapter, we looked at life processes involved in the maintenance functions in living organisms. There, we had started with a notion we all have, that if we see something moving, it is alive. Some of... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 6 CHAPTER, 6.1.1 What happens in Reflex Actions?, 6.1.2 Human Brain, 6.1.3 How are these Tissues protected?. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Life Processes is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that science 100 Control and Coordination 6 CHAPTER In the previous chapter, we looked at life processes involved in the maintenance functions in living organisms.

The text explains that there, we had started with a notion we all have, that if we see something moving, it is alive.

The text explains that some of these movements are in fact the result of growth, as in plants.

The text explains that a seed germinates and grows, and we can see that the seedling moves over the course of a few days, it pushes soil aside and comes out.

The text explains that but if its growth were to be stopped, these movements would not happen.

The text explains that some movements, as in many animals and some plants, are not connected with growth.

The text explains that a cat running, children playing on swings, buffaloes chewing cud – these are not movements caused by growth.

The text explains that why do we associate such visible movements with life?

The text explains that a possible answer is that we think of movement as a response to a change in the environment of the organism.

The text explains that the cat may be running because it has seen a mouse.

The text explains that not only that, we also think of movement as an attempt by living organisms to use changes in their environment to their advantage.

The text explains that children try to get pleasure and fun out of swinging.

The text explains that buffaloes chew cud to help break up tough food so as to be able to digest it better.

The text explains that when bright light is focussed on our eyes or when we touch a hot object, we detect the change and respond to it with movement in order to protect ourselves.

The text explains that if we think a bit more about this, it becomes apparent that all this movement, in response to the environment, is carefully controlled.

The text explains that each kind of a change in the environment evokes an appropriate movement in response.

The text explains that in other words, living organisms must use systems providing control and coordination.

The text explains that 1 ANIMALS – NERVOUS SYSTEM ANIMALS – NERVOUS SYSTEM ANIMALS – NERVOUS SYSTEM ANIMALS – NERVOUS SYSTEM ANIMALS – NERVOUS SYSTEM In animals, such control and coordination are provided by nervous and muscular tissues, which we have studied in Class IX.

The text explains that figure 6.1 Figure 6.1 Figure 6.1 Figure 6.1 Figure 6.1 (a) Structure of neuron, (b) Neuromuscular junction (a) (b)

The text explains that an important question then is, how long will it take us to think all this?

The text explains that rather than having to think about the sensation of heat, if the nerves that detect heat were to be connected to the nerves that move muscles in a simpler way, the process of detecting the signal or the input and responding to it by an output action might be completed quickly.

The text explains that of course, reflex arcs have evolved in animals because the thinking process of the brain is not fast enough.

The text explains that so it is quite likely that reflex arcs have evolved as efficient ways of functioning in the absence of true thought processes.

The text explains that control and Coordination 103 Can you now trace the sequence of events which occur when a bright light is focussed on your eyes?

The text explains that the brain and spinal cord constitute the central nervous system (Fig.

The text explains that this is the second way in which the nervous system communicates with the muscles.

The text explains that the communication between the central nervous system and the other parts of the body is facilitated by the peripheral nervous system consisting of cranial nerves arising from the brain and spinal nerves arising from the spinal cord.

The text explains that all these involuntary actions including blood pressure, salivation and vomiting are controlled by the medulla in the hind-brain.

The text explains that if you run your hand down the middle of your back, you will feel a hard, bumpy structure.

The text explains that so far, we have been talking about nervous tissue, and how it collects information, sends it around the body, processes information, makes decisions based on information, and conveys decisions to muscles for action.

The text explains that so the next question is, how do muscle cells change their shape?

The text explains that 6.2 COORDINA 6.2 COORDINA 6.2 COORDINA 6.2 COORDINA 6.2 COORDINATION IN PL TION IN PL TION IN PL TION IN PL TION IN PL ANTS ANTS ANTS ANTS ANTS Animals have a nervous system for controlling and coordinating the activities of the body.

The text explains that but plants have neither a nervous system nor muscles.

The text explains that on the other hand, the directional movement of a seedling is caused by growth.

The text explains that more commonly, plants respond to stimuli slowly by growing in a particular direction.

The text explains that because this growth is directional, it appears as if the plant is moving.

The text explains that control and Coordination 107 Environmental triggers such as light, or gravity will change the directions that plant parts grow in.

The text explains that these directional, or tropic, movements can be either towards the stimulus, or away from it.

The text explains that the roots of a plant always grow downwards while the shoots usually grow upwards and away from the earth.

The text explains that this upward and downward growth of shoots and roots, respectively, in response to the pull of earth or gravity is, obviously, geotropism (Fig.

The text explains that can we think of examples of these kinds of directional growth movements?

The text explains that one example of chemotropism is the growth of pollen tubes towards ovules, about which we will learn more when we examine the reproductive processes of living organisms.

The text explains that even in animal bodies, there are carefully controlled directions to growth.

The text explains that our arms and fingers grow in certain directions, not haphazardly.

The text explains that leave it undisturbed in this condition for a few days. n Have the old parts of the shoot and root changed direction? n Are there differences in the direction of the new growth? n What can we conclude from this activity?

The text explains that figure 6.5 Figure 6.5 Figure 6.5 Figure 6.5 Figure 6.5 Response of the plant to the direction of light Figure 6.6 Figure 6.6 Figure 6.6 Figure 6.6 Figure 6.6 Plant showing geotropism

The text explains that adrenaline is secreted directly into the blood and carried to different parts of the body.

The text explains that the blood to the digestive system and skin is reduced due to contraction of muscles around small arteries in these organs.

The text explains that such animal hormones are part of the endocrine system which constitutes a second way of control and coordination in our body.

The text explains that remember that plants have hormones that control their directional growth.

The text explains that the gap between two neurons is called a (a) dendrite.

The text explains that what is the function of receptors in our body?

Source: NCERT Class 10 Science, Chapter 6: Life Processes

schemes.name: In
schemes.point: In keeping with the general principles of body organisation in multicellular organisms, specialised tissues are used to provide these control and coordination activities. (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch6 pg 1)

schemes.name: Writing
schemes.point: Writing, talking, moving a chair, clapping at the end of a programme are examples of voluntary actions which are based on deciding what to do next. (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch6 pg 3)

schemes.name: They
schemes.point: They are synthesised at places away from where they act and simply diffuse to the area of action. (NCERT Class 10 Science Ch6 pg 8)

Do not add outside examples to Life Processes; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use 6 CHAPTER and 6.1.1 What happens in Reflex Actions? to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

When we want to talk to our friends in class, we whisper, rather than shouting loudly.

Therefore, such controlled movement must be connected to the recognition of various events in the environment, followed by only the correct movement in response.
